title: Synthesis of miktoarm star (?-star) polymers
abstract:
HIGHLIGHT
Synthesis of Miktoarm Star (m-Star) Polymers
NIKOS HADJICHRISTIDIS
University of Athens, Department of Chemistry, Panepistimiopolis, Zografou,
157 71 Athens, Greece
Received 20 October 1998; accepted 30 October 1998
ABSTRACT: The synthetic strat-
egies available for the synthesis of
miktoarm star (m-star) polymers
with molecular weight, chemical,
or topological asymmetry are re-
viewed. All strategies are based on
functional living polymer chains
and linking agents. Although each
strategy has its weak and strong
points, it seems that anionic poly-
merization combined with chlo-
rosilane methodology yields the
widest variety of model single and
double m-stars. These novel archi-
tectures open new horizons in
polymer science and technology.
© 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Inc. J Polym
Sci A: Polym Chem 37: 857–871, 1999
Keywords: molecular architecture;
miktoarm stars (m-stars); molecular
weight asymmetry; chemical asym-
metry; topological asymmetry; living
polymers; linking agents; morphology
Nikos Hadjichristidis: B.Sc. University of Athens, Greece (1966); Ph.D.
University of Lie`ge, Belgium (1971); D.Sc. University of Athens, Greece
(1978); Postdoctoral research: University of Lie`ge with Professor V.
Desreux (1971–1972) and National Research Council of Canada with Dr.
J. Roovers (1972–1973); Lecturer (1973), Assistant Professor (1982),
Associate Professor (1985), Full Professor (1988), Director of Industrial
Chemistry Laboratory (since 1994), and Chairman of the Chemistry
Department (1991–1995) of the University of Athens; Visiting Scientist,
University of Lie`ge (Summers 1974, 1975); Visiting Research Ofﬁcer,
NRC of Canada (Summer 1976); Visiting Scientist, University of Akron
at Dr. L. Fetters’ Laboratory (summers 1977 through 1982); Distin-
guished Visiting Scientist, NRC of Canada (1983); Visiting Professor,
Exxon Research and Engineering Co, NJ, since 1984, every year 1–2
months. President of the European Polymer Federation (1995–1996);
Member of the National Advisory Research Council since 1994; Presi-
dent of the State Highest Chemical Board since 1995; Academy of
Athens Award for Chemistry (1989) and Empirikion Award for Sciences
(1994); More than 200 papers in refereed scientiﬁc journals.
NIKOS
HADJICHRISTIDIS
857
This article is dedicated to the memory of Reimund Stadler
Journal of Polymer Science: Part A: Polymer Chemistry, Vol. 37, 857–871 (1999)
© 1999 John Wiley & Sons, Inc.
CCC 0887-624X/99/070857-15
 INTRODUCTION
Polymers with narrow molecular and compositional (in
the case of copolymers) dispersity and with well-deﬁned
architecture are essential for establishing structure/prop-
erties relationships. These relationships are necessary to
achieve one of the ultimate goals of polymer chemistry:
designing molecules with predetermined properties.
Star polymers, consisting of several identical linear
chains linked together at one end of each chain (regular
or symmetric stars), attracted the attention of scientists
because they constitute the simplest form of branching.
The earliest attempt to synthesize star molecules was that
of Schaefgen and Flory in 1948,1 who by polymerizing
«-caprolactam in the presence of either cyclohexanon-
etetrapropionic or dicyclohexanoneoctacarboxylic acid
obtained tetra- and octachain star-shaped polyamides.
Fourteen years later, Morton and coworkers2 took
advantage of the living character of anionic polymeriza-
tion to synthesize four-arm star polystyrenes (PS) by
neutralizing living polystyryllithium with tetrachlorosi-
lane. Although the produced materials were mixtures of
three- and four-arm PS, this work eventually led to the
preparation of star polymers with up to 128 arms.
In 1963, Oroﬁno and Wenger3 ﬁrst used tri(chlorom-
ethyl)benzene as a linking agent to prepare the three-arm
star PS. Mayer4 used 1,2,4,5-tetra(chloromethyl)benzene
to prepare four-arm star di- and triblock copolymers of
styrene and isoprene. It is difﬁcult to extend the func-
tionality ( f ) of the stars beyond f 5 6 with chlorom-
ethylbenzene derivatives.5 Other compounds used as
linking agents, for example, cyclic trimer of phosphoni-
trilic chloride,6 2,4,6-tri(allyloxy)triazine,7 1,1,4,4-tetra-
phenyl-1,4-bis(diallyloxytriazine)butane,8 tin tetrachlo-
ride,9 and phosphorus trichloride10 suffer the same dis-
advantage. Decker and Rempp11 were the ﬁrst to
demonstrate the validity of divinylbenzene (DVB) as a
linking agent by preparing and properly characterizing
PS stars with 6 to 15 arms. The DVB method was
apparently ﬁrst alluded to by Milkovich,12 but unfortu-
nately, in his patent there was no clear indication that
star-branched
polymers
were
prepared.
The
DVB
method does not allow one to accurately predict the
number of the arms of the stars, not does it yield poly-
mers with a uniform number of arms.13 As a result,
multifunctional chlorosilane compounds became the
linking agents of choice. Table I summarizes the evolu-
tion of the synthesis of symmetric star polymers by the
chlorosilane linking agents.
In 1989, Roovers and collaborators24 prepared star
polybutadienes with 200 and 270 arms. The linking
agents was a hydrosilylated, low molecular-weight linear
or star 1,2-polybutadiene. Although the chlorosilane
method is limited to styrenic and dienic polymers, the
exhaustive study of their properties has led to important
conclusions about the inﬂuence of the star architecture.25
These model polymers have also been used to test the
corresponding theory.26
Many other interesting systems using cationic, group
transfer, or living ring-opening metathesis polymer-
ization have now been developed, leading to new sym-
metric stars of vinylethers:27–31 isobutylene,32–36 methyl
methacrylate,37 and norbornene.38–39
Recently, the synthesis of megamolecules40 with un-
equal arms (molecular weight asymmetry) or with chemi-
cally different arms (chemical asymmetry) has been
achieved (Chart 1a and 1b). The term miktoarm stars (com-
ing from the greek word mikto´§, meaning mixed) was
adopted for the stars with chemical asymmetry. The term
heteroarm stars (hetero coming from the greek word
«´t«ro§, meaning other) is not appropriate for this class of
polymers, because it does not convey the concept of a group
composed of dissimilar elements. Stars with molecular
weight asymmetry can be considered miktoarm homopoly-

or star 1,2-polybutadiene. Although the chlorosilane

transfer, or living ring-opening metathesis polymer-
ization have now been developed, leading to new sym-
metric stars of vinylethers:27–31 isobutylene,32–36 methyl

equal arms (molecular weight asymmetry) or with chemi-
cally different arms (chemical asymmetry) has been
achieved (Chart 1a and 1b). The term miktoarm stars (com-
ing from the greek word mikto´§, meaning mixed) was
adopted for the stars with chemical asymmetry. The term

«´t«ro§, meaning other) is not appropriate for this class of
polymers, because it does not convey the concept of a group

Evolution of the Synthesis of Symmetric Star Polymers by Chlorosilanes
Hadjichristidis, Guyot, Fetters18

J. POLYM. SCI. PART A: POLYM. CHEM.: VOL. 37 (1999)
mers. Stars having arms of similar chemical nature but
different end groups also belong to this category. Finally,
topologically asymmetric stars are also m-stars. They con-
sist of diblock copolymer arms that are attached by different
ends to the star center. The synthetic efforts leading to
miktoarm stars (m-stars) are highlighted. A few examples of
the striking inﬂuence of star structure on the morphology of
block co- and terpolymers will also be given.
SYNTHESIS OF MIKTOARM STAR (m-STAR)
Although there are several speciﬁc methods, two general
strategies have been developed for the synthesis of
Miktoarm star (m-star) polymers.
Miktoarm star (m-star) polymers.
Double miktoarm star polymers.
m-stars. Both of them use living polymers (anionic or
cationic). The ﬁrst one is based on divinyl compounds.
They are either homopolymerizable (e.g., divinylben-
zene) or nonhomopolymerizable (e.g., double diphenyl
ethylene, DDPE). The second method uses multifunc-
tional substitution reactions on appropriate linking
Anionic Polymerization/Divinylbenzene
(Homopolymerizable Linking Agents)
The use of divinylbenzene (DVB) for the synthesis
of miktoarm stars was ﬁrst recognized by Es-
chwey and Burchard41 and developed mainly by
Rempp and colleagues.42–45 The general reactions
The living precursor ALi polymerizes a small
amount of DVB,46 leading to the formation of a
star molecule bearing within its core (microgel
nodule of DVB) a number of active sites, which is
theoretically equal to the number of incorporated
A arms of the star polymer. Subsequent addition
of another monomer, B, or the same monomer A,
yields a m-star copolymer or homopolymer.
The growing B arms have anionic sites at their
outer ends, thus providing the possibility of react-
ing with electrophilic compounds or other mono-
mers towards the preparation of end-functional-
ized stars or star-block copolymers. Because of its
simplicity, this method can be carried out in an
inert atmosphere, thereby avoiding the use of
highly demanding and time-consuming vacuum
The DVB method has been applied to the
synthesis of m-star copolymers of AnBn type, with
A arm polystyrene and B arm poly(tert-butyl
methacrylate),43 poly(tert-butyl acrylate),44 – 46
poly(ethylene oxide),47 or poly(2-vinyl pyridine).48
Usually, n varies between 6 and 20. PS m-star
homopolymers of the type AnA9n were also pre-
The DVB method is characterized by several
disadvantages, the foremost being the architec-
tural limitations. Only stars with equal number
of arms different in chemical nature or molecular
weight AnBn can be prepared. In fact, because the
polymerization of DVB by the living chains is not
controllable, n is an average value that is inﬂu-
enced by several parameters. Speciﬁcally, n in-
creases with decreasing molecular weight of the
precursor A and the molar ratio of the DVB to
living chains. Another disadvantage is that the B
arms cannot be isolated and characterized inde-
pendently. Finally, reaction of the living ends of
the remaining double bond of the DVB nodule can
lead to the formation of loops (intramolecular re-
action) or networks (intermolecular reaction).
Cationic Polymerization/Divinylethers
(Homopolymerizable Linking Agents)
In principle, the DVB strategy is equally applica-
ble to living chains produced by cationic polymer-
ization. Higashimura and coworkers50 were the
ﬁrst to prepare m-stars based on the living poly-
merization of vinylethers.51,52
The reaction of the macrocation A1..I2..ZnI2
with a small amount of the divinyl ether (Scheme
2) leads to a star having living sites in its core,
that can polymerize the second vinyl ether. Divi-
nyl ethers having long and rigid spacers between
the two vinyl groups proved to be more efﬁcient
coupling agents. The use of divinyl compounds in
cationic polymerization suffers the same disad-
vantage as in anionic polymerization. Neverthe-
less, the possibility of using monomers that can-
not be polymerized anionically makes the method
attractive and susceptible to several applications.
For example, alkaline hydrolysis of the ester
groups of the second monomer to COOH groups
leads to an amphiphilic m-star.
J. POLYM. SCI. PART A: POLYM. CHEM.: VOL. 37 (1999)
Anionic Polymerization/Double Diphenylethylenes
(Nonhomopolymerizable Linking Agents)
Ho¨cker and Latterman,53 in 1976, recognized the
usefulness of the addition of living chains to non-
were the ﬁrst to propose that 4m-stars could be
obtained by adding living chains to bis(1-phenyl-
vinyl)benzenes followed by a subsequent addition
of monomer. In 1978, Szwarc and coworkers54
spectroscopically studied the kinetics of addition
of PSLi to several divinyl compounds in benzene.
They found that in the case of the para-double
ethenyl)benzene, the ratio of the rate constants of
the ﬁrst to the second addition was 13. In 1983,
Leitz and Ho¨cker55 reported that the reaction of
two mol of sec-BuLi with the meta-double diphe-
nylethylene (MDDPE), 1,3-bis(1-phenylethenyl)-
benzene, proceeds rapidly and efﬁciently to pro-
duce a dilithium initiator. The ratio of the rate
constants of the ﬁrst and second addition is al-
Quirk and coworkers56 have further developed
this “living linking method” for the synthesis of
three-m and four-m-stars. The general reactions
are given in Schemes 3 and 4. PDDPE is usually
used for the synthesis of m-A2B and m-ABC,
whereas the MDDPE is for A2B2 m-stars. Re-
cently, Quirk’s group tried to extend the method
to six-m-stars by using a triple diphenylethylene,
1,3,5-tris(1-phenylethyl)benzene.57
The crucial point of the living linking proce-
dure is the control of the stoichiometry of the
reaction between the living A chains and the
DDPE; otherwise, a mixture of star and linear
polymers is produced. A major problem is that the
rate constants of initiation of the two new active
sites differ, resulting in a bimodal distribution. To
overcome this problem, polar compounds have to
be added. It is well known that they dramatically
affect the microstructure of the polydienes that
are formed in the later stages. However, the ad-
dition of lithium sec-butoxide to the living coupled
DDPE derivative, prior to the addition of the
diene monomer, was found to produce monomodal
well-deﬁned m-stars with high 1,4 content. Again,
the B arms cannot be isolated from the reaction
mixture and characterized separately. Neverthe-
less, Quirk’s method is valuable for the synthesis
Cationic Polymerization/Bis-Diphenylethylenes
(Nonhomopolymerizable Linking Agents)
Faust and coworkers58 applied the living linking
method of Quirk to the coupling reaction of living
polyisobutylene with MDDPE, 2,2-bis(diphenyl-
ethenyl)propane (BDTEP). Reaction of living PIB
with MDDPE leads to the monoadduct, possibly
due to delocalization of a positive charge over
the meta-substituted benzene. With BDPED and
BDTEP, which have two DPE moieties separated
by an electron-donating spacer, a rapid and quan-
titative coupling reaction was achieved indepen-
dently of the molecular weight of the precursor
PIB. Due to the presence of CH3 groups, the re-
action with BDTEP was more rapid than with
BDPEP. By using BDTEP Faust and colleagues59
were the ﬁrst to prepare 4m-star (PIB)2(PMeVE)2
according to Scheme 5. Before the addition of the
monomer the Lewis acidity should be moderated
to the reactivity of MeVE by adding Ti(OEt)4.
This method is attractive mainly because it
Anionic Polymerization/Chlorosilanes
Regular stars can be synthesized by reacting an
excess of living chain, prepared by anionic poly-
merization, with the appropriate chlorosilane.
The excess of the living polymer is needed to force
the linking reaction to completion. For the syn-
thesis of m-stars, each chlorine atom should be
replaced stepwise by a different chain. To achieve
this goal we must take into consideration the
different reactivity of the living chain ends to-
wards the SiOCl group. It appears that the reac-
tivity is in the order of the charge localization on
the terminal carbon, and in the opposite order of
the local steric hindrance, i.e., BdLi . IsLi . StLi
. DPELi. The reactivity of the living chains is
also affected by the molecular weight, the polarity
of the environment, and the temperature.
Trichloromethylsilane and Tetrachlorosilane
CH3SiCl3 and SiCl4, are appropriate linking
agents for three-m- and four-m-stars. The re-
placement of one Cl only by one chain can be
achieved by adding dropwise the living polymer
into an excess of the chlorosilane. Before the
addition of the second living chain unreacted
chlorosilane is removed to avoid contamination
of the m-stars by the homostars with three
(CH3SiCl3) or four (SiCl4) arms. The above chlo-
(CH3SiCl3, b.p.: 66°C and SiCl4, 57.6°C), can be
easily removed on a vacuum line. This method
was developed by Pennisi and Fetters,60 and
used for the synthesis of three-m-star homopoly-
mers of styrene and butadiene. The general re-
actions are given in Scheme 6.
The excess of A9Li, needed for the completion of
the linking reaction, is eliminated by fraction-
ation as in the case of regular stars. In the case of
PS stars, to ensure complete reaction of the PSLi
with the macrocoupling agent ASi(CH3)Cl2, the
living PS is end capped with a few units of buta-
Mays prepared61 one sample of three-m-star
PS(PI)2 by this method. The method was further
developed by our group to all possible combina-
tion of A2B m-stars, where A, B is PS, PI or PB.62
Furthermore by using SiCl4, we have prepared
PS(PI)3 four-m-stars.63 A more sophisticated high
vacuum technique was used to ensure the synthe-
sis of well-deﬁned m-stars.64 A high degree of mo-
lecular and compositional homogeneity was indi-
cated by size exclusion chromatography (SEC),
with a refractive index or UV-detector, low-angle
laser light-scattering (LALLS), membrane (MO),
and vapor-pressure osmometry (VPO) and NMR
spectroscopy. The chlorosilane approach was also
followed for the synthesis of m-stars PS(PI-b-
When only one chlorine needs to be replaced by
a highly reactive living chain, for example, low
molecular-weight PBLi or PI, the living end has to
be transformed into a less reactive carbanion. By
decreasing the reactivity, the selectivity is in-
creased and the replacement of only one chlorine
is achieved. By using DPE, we have prepared
model mono- and difunctional 3m-star PBs66 and
(d-PB)2(PI), (d-PB) (PI)2, where d-PB is deuter-
ated PB,67 having arm molecular weights less
than 10,000. The same goal can be achieved more
simply (avoiding the necessity for end capping) by
linking the ﬁrst arm or arms at a temperature low
enough to create selectivity between the succes-
sive steps of replacement of chlorines.68
By using the chlorosilane method four-m-stars
(PS-b-PB)(PB)3 were synthesized by Tsiang.69
Living polybutadiene PB chains were ﬁrst reacted
with SiCl4 in a molar ratio 3 : 1, followed by the
addition of the living diblock (PS-b-PB)Li. The
key step of this method is the successful synthesis
of the (PB)3SiCl intermediate. The high reactivity
of the PBLi chain end poses questions about the
J. POLYM. SCI. PART A: POLYM. CHEM.: VOL. 37 (1999)
purity of this polymer, because several byprod-
ucts, such as (PB)2SiCl2, (PB)4Si, PBSiCl3, can be
formed in the ﬁrst step. SEC analysis was per-
formed to monitor the reaction sequence. It is
obvious that the method developed by Tsiang is
very demanding with regard to the stoichiometry
of the reagents. The byproducts are almost impos-
In the preparation of 3m-ABC terpolymers two
chlorine atoms have to be consecutively and se-
lectively displaced. The ﬁrst one can be displaced
by excess of CH3SiCl3. The second chlorine is
displaced by titration. The synthesis of 3m-star
terpolymer of St, Is, and Bd is given in Scheme
The order of linking of the different arms to the
silane plays an essential role. The most sterically
hindered chain, PSLi, has to be added by titra-
tion. The least sterically hindered chain end,
PBLi, has to be added at the end of the synthesis.
The whole procedure was monitored by SEC, as
Using the same route asymmetric AA9B mik-
toarm stars were also prepared.70 These are stars
having two chemically identical A arms but of
different molecular weights. In this case, the B
chain is not grafted at the center of the A chain as
in the case of the symmetric A2B stars.
The synthesis of 4m-star copolymer (PS)2(PI)2
was accomplished in a similar way.71 The PSLi was
added in two separate steps (excess and titration) to
have the maximum control over the polymer archi-
Only one miktoarm star quaterpolymer has
branches PS, poly(4-methyl styrene), (P4MeS),
PI, and PB.71 The reaction sequence for the prep-
aration of this miktoarm star is presented in
The characteristic of this method is that two of
the arms were incorporated by titration. PS was
chosen to react ﬁrst with an excess of SiCl4, fol-
lowed after the evaporation of the excess silane,
by the titration with the more sterically hindered
P4MeS. The moderately hindered PILi anion was
then added by titration. The least sterically hin-
dered PBLi anion was used to complete the link-
Xie and Xia were the ﬁrst to prepare a 4m-star
A2B2.72 Living PS chains were reacted with SiCl4
in a molar ratio 2 : 1 leading to the formation of
the two-arm product. The two remaining SiOCl
bonds can be used for the linking reaction of living
PEO chains. The process is facilitated by the in-
SEC chromatograms taken during the dif-
ferent stages of the synthesis of a m-ABC miktoarm star
creased steric hindrance of the living PS chain
ends. It is, indeed, very difﬁcult to prepare the
three- or four-arm PS stars. From this point of
view the control of the stoichiometry is less im-
portant in this speciﬁc case. Using CH3SiCl3 in-
stead of SiCl4, A2B miktoarm stars were also pre-
A new class of asymmetric stars, the topologi-
cally asymmetric star block copolymers, have
been prepared by the chlorosilane method using
SiCl4.73 These polymers are four-arm stars, with
each of the arms being a diblock copolymer of
isoprene and styrene. Two blocks are connected to
the star’s center with their PS end and the other
two blocks with their PI end. The living diblock
(PI-b-PS)Li solution was added dropwise to a stoi-
chiometric amount of SiCl4 until two arms are
linked to the silane. This step was monitored by
SEC, and is similar to a titration process. The
difunctional macromolecular linking agent was
then reacted with a small excess of the living
diblock (PS-b-PI)Li for the preparation of the in-
verse star block copolymers. The order of the ad-
dition was again dictated by steric factors.
1,2-Bis(trichlorosilyl)ethane (SOCl6) was used
for the synthesis of 6m-stars PS(PI)5.74 The stoi-
chiometric addition of PSLi to the SOCl6 was
chosen in this case, instead of excess of SOCl6,
because unreacted chlorosilane cannot be re-
moved by distillation. The reaction sequence is
Living PS chains were reacted with 1,2-bis(tri-
chlorosilyl)ethane in a ratio 5 : L. Dropwise addi-
tion of the living polymer solution into the vigor-
ously stirred solution of the linking agent was
performed to minimize multiple coupling prod-
ucts. Under these conditions 15% of the dimeric
capped PS was then reacted with excess of PILi.
Fractionation techniques were employed to iso-
late the desired polymer. An alternative method
would be to use an excess SOCl6 and to remove it
with a nonsolvent for PS (e.g., hexane) and ﬁlter
the solution of hexane with the unused SOCl6.
However, this procedure poses technical prob-
lems, because it has to be carried out under vac-
uum and repeated several times.
Hexadecachlorosilane (SOCl16) and
Model 16m-star copolymers with eight PS and
eight PI arms, PS8PI8, were synthesized using a
dendritic carbosilane with 16 SiOCl bonds.75 Liv-
ing PS chains were reacted with the linking agent
in a molar ratio of 8 : 1 for the preparation of
the eight-arm star. Even a slight excess of PSLi
(; 5%) can be used without the incorporation of
more than eight arms due to the steric hindrance
of the already attached PS chain on each Si atom.
A small excess of PILi was ﬁnally added to pre-
pare the ﬁnal product. These copolymers are
called Vergina star copolymers after their simi-

3 17 cm) from the Great Tomb of the King Philippos
discovered by the late Professor M. Andronicos, in Ver-
gina, a place in the Northern Greece close to Thessal-

Macedonian Dynasty (Thessaloniki Archeological Mu-

Philippos of Macedonia in Vergina, a place close
By using the same methodology and a chlorosi-
lane with 64 peripheral SiOCl groups, Roovers76
prepared a 64m-star copolymer, A32A932 of PB.
Complex Architectures (Double m-Stars)
Double m-stars have been prepared by selectively
coupling with difunctional living instead of mono-
functional chains. The structures obtained are
given in Chart 2. As an example, the synthetic
procedure of the super H-shaped block copoly-
mer77 is outlined in Scheme 10.
By applying the chlorosilane approach, we were
able to prepare model 1,4-polybutadienes A2A,
AA9A0, A2A9A2, A3A9A3, and A5A9A5 and by hy-
drogenation, the corresponding branched polyeth-
Cationic Polymerization/Linking Agents
Although regular star homo- and block copoly-
mers, based on cationic polymerization and efﬁ-
cient linking agents have been prepared,79 the
synthesis of m-stars using the above methodology
has not appeared yet in the literature.
3m-Star Copolymers of A2B Type
A2B stars with two PS arms and one poly(2-vinyl
pyridine) (P2VP) arm, (PS)2(P2VP), were pre-
pared by Khan et al.80 Living PS chains were
linked to methyldichlorosilane, CH3SiCl2H, to
produce the two arms of the star. In another re-
actor, living P2VP was terminated with allyl bro-
mide. The complete m-star was produced through
hydrosilylation addition of the allyl and SiH
groups. Due to incomplete hydrosilylation, the
m-stars were characterized by relatively high
polydispersities (1.33 to 1.50).
Anionic polymerization techniques and naph-
thalene chemistry were used by Teyssie´ et al. to
prepare A2B miktoarm stars, where A is poly(eth-
ylene oxide) (PEO), and B is PS, PI, poly(a-methyl
styrene) or poly(t-butyl styrene).81
A special technique was employed by Naka et
al. for the preparation of A2B stars; A being PEO,
and B polyoxazoline (POX) by forming Ru(III)
complexes with bipyridyl terminated polymers.82
Star polymers with several PS branches and only
one poly(2-vinyl naphthalene), (PVN), branch
were prepared by Takano et al. by anionic poly-
merization techniques.83 Sequential anionic block
copolymerization of (4-vinylphenyl) dimethylvi-
nylsilane (VS) and VN was employed. The double
bonds attached to silicon have to remain unaf-
fected during the polymerization of VS. This was
accomplished with a K counterion in the THF and
short polymerization times. The PVS block with
the unreacted double bonds was used as a multi-
functional linking agent. Subsequent addition of
living PS chains produced miktoarm stars of the
Similar structures of the ABn type were pre-
pared by Wang and Roovers, A being PS, and B
PB or P2VP.84 Due to the much higher molecular
weight of the PS arm, these m-stars were called
umbrella copolymers. The reaction sequence for
the preparation of the PS(PB)n copolymers is
given in Scheme 11. Butadiene was polymerized
anionically in the presence of dipiperidinoethane
(dipip) followed by the addition of styrene. Hy-
drosilylation chemistry was employed to add the
1,2-PB double bonds. Subsequent addition of 1,4-
PBLi or P2VPK leads to the formation of the
umbrella copolymers. The limited control excer-
sized over the hydrosilylation reaction means
that the number of the arms cannot be accurately
Roovers and collaborators85 also succeeded
in preparing umbrella-star copolymers [(PS-u-
PI)n]m (Chart 3). The synthesis is based on the
reaction of (1,2-PB-b-PS)Li, with chlorosilane
having 32 peripheral SiOCl bonds followed by
hydrosilylation of the PB double bonds and reac-
m-Star Copolymers of the AnBm Type
Ishizu and Kuwahara86 have developed a mac-
romonomer technique for the synthesis of mik-
toarm stars of the type AnBm. PS and PI mac-
romonomers with end vinyl groups were anioni-
cally copolymerized in benzene solutions with
n-BuLi. The products can be considered miktoarm
stars of the type AnBm, as evidenced by their
solution and solid-state properties.
Diblock macromonomers with central vinyl
groups were used for the synthesis of (PS)n(PtBu-
MA)n m-stars.87,88 The macromonomers were pre-
pared by sequential anionic polymerization of
styrene, 1,4-divinylbenzene (DVB), and t-butyl
methacrylate. The DVB monomer was left to re-
act with the living PS chains for short times (; 5
min) so that only a few DVB units are incorpo-
rated at the end of the PS chains, and the forma-
tion of PS stars is avoided. Free radical polymer-
ization in solution and in bulk using AIBN as an
initiator, tetramethylthiuram as a photosensi-
tizer, and ethylene glycol dimethacrylate as a
crosslinking agent was carried out for the synthe-
sis of the m-stars. A similar experiment was per-
formed using PS-b-P2VP diblocks having central
Finally, a cyclophosphazene derivative was
used as a linking agent to produce m-stars con-
sisting of PS and Nylon-6 arms.89
m-Star Terpolymers of the ABC Type
Fujimoto et al. prepared (PS)(PDMS)(PtBuMA)
m-stars,90 as described in Scheme 12. The lithium
salt of the p-(dimethylhydroxy)silyl-diphenyleth-
ylene was used as the initiator for the polymer-
ization of hexamethylcyclotrisiloxane (D3). Living
PS chains were reacted with the end double bond
of the diphenylethylene, followed by the anionic
J. POLYM. SCI. PART A: POLYM. CHEM.: VOL. 37 (1999)
The PDMS was characterized by a rather
broad molecular weight distribution (I ; 1.4), and
a fractionation was performed before the follow-
ing synthetic steps. The polymethacrylate branch
cannot be isolated and studied independently.
A similar synthetic route was adopted by Sta-
dler et al. for the synthesis of (PS)(PB)(PMMA)
stars,91 as shown in Scheme 13.
Living PS chains were end capped with p-bro-
momethyldiphenylethylene. The next step in-
volved the linking of living PB chains, prepared in
THF at 210°C, to the end double bond. This pro-
duces a new active center that was used to initi-
ate the polymerization of MMA.
The chlorosilane method was also used by Had-
jichristidis et al. for the synthesis of m-stars with
PS, PI, and PMMA arms.92,93 The reaction se-
quence is presented in Scheme 14. The monofunc-
tional macromolecular linking agent (PS)(PI)-
(CH3)SiCl was prepared using procedures similar
This linking agent was reacted with a stoichi-
ometric amount of the dilithium initiator pre-
pared from 1,1-diphenylethylene (DPE) and Li.
The remaining active center was used to polymer-
ize MMA in THF at 278°C. According to this
procedure, the PMMA branch cannot be isolated
and cannot be characterized independently.
A technique similar to Stadler’s was em-
ployed for the synthesis of miktoarm stars hav-
ing PS, PEO, poly(«-caprolactone) (PCL), or

After deprotection and transformation of the

ring-opening polymerization of the third mono-

6m-Star Copolymers of A2B4 Type
Very recently, Hirao and coworkers95 have pro-
posed a promising methodology for the synthesis
of three-, four-, ﬁve-, and six-arm regular and
m-stars. Anionic living chains react with specially
designed terminators with a predetermined num-
ber of methoxy benzyl groups. The methoxy
group, followed by reaction with another living
chain. As an example of the concept the synthesis
of a 6m-star A2B4 is given in Scheme 15.
MORPHOLOGY OF 6m-STAR COPOLYMERS
The morphology (and consequently the proper-
ties) of a given linear block copolymer are con-
trolled by the volume fraction of the components.
However, in almost all applications it would be
desirable to control the microphase morphology
and volume fraction separately. This can be
achieved by changing the linear to a nonlinear
block copolymer architecture. The m-star copoly-
mers shift the borders of the classical morphology
map or form new morphological structures.96 As
an example, the PS-b-PI(PS: 40% by vol) has a
(PI)2PS and (PI)3PS block copolymers, with the
same overall molecular weight and composition,
form respectively hexagonally packed cylinders
and spheres in a BCC array (Fig. 3).
An inverse four-arm star block copolymer of
styrene and isoprene (PS: 50% by vol.) showed an
ordered bicontinuous double-diamond morphol-
ogy73 never seen before in the strong segregation
The effect of architecture is even more striking
when the m-star PS, PI, and PMMA and linear
terpolymer morphologies are compared.97–100 The
star architecture gives molecules the ability to
choose which pairs of arm blocks arrange adja-
cently so as to minimize the unfavorable contact
energies.101,102 The resulting extraordinary mor-
Simulation of the unit cell of the Ordered
Bicontinuous Double Diamond (OBDD) morphology,
that was observed at the inverse m-star copolymers.
The unit cell of OBDD has dimensions on the scale of a
few hundred Angstroms, cubic symmetry, and consists
of eight tetrahedric elements that every four of them
form two independent networks. These two networks
are continuous, periodic in three dimensions, do not
intersect and are formed by the phase of one compo-
nents (PS), whereas the other component (PI) forms the
Schematic representation of the microdo-
main morphology of the m-SIM stars (% vol.: PS: 20.5,
PI: 25, PMMA: 54.5, and PS: 22.3, PI: 27, PMMA: 50.5),
which exhibit hexagonally packed cylinders of PI with a
concentric PS annulus in a matrix of PMMA. The chain
conformation of the three blocks and the location of the
junction point are also presented. The PMMA arms are
partially mixed within the PS domain, and the junction
points are distributed over the PI/PS intermaterial di-
J. POLYM. SCI. PART A: POLYM. CHEM.: VOL. 37 (1999)
phologies (Figs. 5 and 6) have potential applica-
tions in high-strength composites, conducting
polymers, separation membranes, and nanostruc-
The solution and bulk properties of m-stars
have been reviewed recently.103
Living polymerization procedures and linking
agents have proven to be efﬁcient to prepare
m-stars with arms of different molecular weight,
chemical nature, or topology. Most of these mik-
toarm stars (m-stars) have well-deﬁned structures
with high degree of molecular weight and compo-
sitional homogeneity. Despite the fact that the
chlorosilane methodology is time consuming, a
careful manipulation of the different coupling re-
actions leads to a wide variety of model simple
and double miktoarm stars. These novel poly-
meric architectures have revealed a rich variety
of static and dynamic properties.
